entry.text: The hardware store is a cathedral of rust and opportunity — thirty-foot shelving runs stretching into a gloom lit only by the holes in the roof, the floor a mosaic of burst paint cans, scattered fasteners, and the kind of heavy silence that suggests the place has been claimed. A hand-painted sign nailed to the entrance beam reads: LEAVE SOMETHING OR LEAVE FAST.

choices.label: Leave a tin of food and browse openly
Slip in through the loading dock quietly

main_floor.text: The main floor has been sorted — not looted, sorted. Someone has organized the remaining stock into rough categories: fuel canisters, cable, pipe fittings, filters. It is the work of a careful, practical mind. Whatever they need this material for, it is ongoing. You have room to take what you can justify without triggering whoever left that sign.

choices.label: Check the locked tool crib at the back

tool_crib.text: The tool crib is a {{crib_state}}. Heavy gear — generators, compressors, welding rigs — sits on pallets under dust sheets, untouched, too bulky to carry. But the upper shelving holds smaller items in sealed cases: filters, seals, hand tools in factory packaging. You fill what space remains in your pack, moving quietly in case that sign's author is somewhere nearby listening.

disrespect.text: You left nothing, and someone was watching. A slingshot bolt cracks the shelf beside your head — a warning shot, but barely. A gravel-deep voice from the rafters tells you to put down half your pack and get out. You do.

caught_sneaking.text: A trip-wire attached to a stack of metal pipe sends the whole column crashing down behind you. Your ankle twists hard under the impact of a rolling section.

alarm_rig.text: The tool crib door is wired to a car horn mounted on the ceiling. The blast goes on for thirty seconds — every living thing in the block knows your location. You have seconds to grab and move.

chemical_spill.text: A shelf of unlabeled solvent drums has corroded through at the bottom. You step into a pooled slick and breathe the fumes before you can cover your face. Your lungs burn for the next hour.
